OP

>rc buys 7,780,000 shares of BBBY, and $60-$80 strike calls worth 1,670,100 shares in March 2022

>rc sells shares and calls of BBBY in august 2022

>rc has a sitdown with a famous corporate raider, Carl Icahn in october 2022

>rc offers 400 million for BBBY in December 2022

>pitchbook (best M&A software suite in the world, costing 5 figures + per year) lists BBBY(now known as BBBYQ or DK-Butterfly-1) as sold via a leveraged buyout on January 13 2023

>a default on the ABL with JPM happened on the same day that pitchbook shows a leveraged buyout, the terms of that ABL specify that a default is triggered if a change of control happens, a change of control is defined as an acquisition of 40% or more of the voting power by any person or group.

>one month later, February 2023 RC tweets “someone tell the government to stop shooting down my balloons”, alluding to bad actors delaying the Bed Bath m&a

>the GameStop CEO then became a BBBYQ chapter 11 creditor and debtor

>Larry cheng (who helped RC build Chewy, and is a current boardmember at GameStop, and a close personal friend to RC) went onto theppshow podcast (which is dedicated to BBBY recovery theories) 8 months after BBBY filed for chapter 11, and 2 months after shares were cancelled

>OverStock - who is the most famous company to battle illegal naked shorts, who even released a crypto dividend 5 years ago - purchased the BBBY IP, and owns 55% of TZero - the only approved blockchain entity who can issue debt instruments and securities

>OverStock - whos now traded under ticker BBBY - has issued warrants with dates that match GME's warrants, and with a CUSIP-6 that matches BBBYQ's CUSIP-6, proving BBBYQ has already begun m&a activity with BBBY


REQUIRED READING: bbbwhy.com

>Larry Cheng and Bill Pulte have gone onto theppshow podcast

jake2b started a youtube channel to access quality BBBYQ easier than scrolling twitter posts:

https://www.youtube.com/@jake2b

VOO and Chill

Open thread • Replies: 205 • Updated: 2026-07-09 19:44 UTC

OP

The more I look into investing and compare the historical returns of certain ETFs / individual stocks it seems like the whole philosophy of “VOO and Chill” is equal parts really smart and retarded
It’s like a no brainer for money but even now as long as the market is in a growth period shouldn’t you at least do QQQ?
It seems so simple that’d you miss a lot of upside but the returns are honestly not bad and extremely consistent
How accurate is this assessment? It’s both so simple and guaranteed that everyone should it but also too safe so you end up missing out on more upside
I’m talking 5-20 years investing here not trading

but biz/anons don't care about being rich when they're older, they want to be rich NOW
greed is a deadly sin

>I think a decade long CAGR comparison was a 5% difference but I’m not sure if CAGR is the best stat to go by?
It changes significantly depending on when you enter the market. Since 2000 there's only a 0.6% difference in favor of QQQ over SPY, the latter outperformed the former for most of that timeframe. If you bought sometime post-2009, however, you would have been better off holding QQQ by far. The moral of the story is keep an eye on things and only buy more volatile assets if it's a growth period. QQQ will continue to be great if you bought a decade ago until the music stops.

item.title: Has Bitcoin become too dependent on ETFs and macro news? — 77 replies — 133.1/hr
item.content:encoded:
Has Bitcoin become too dependent on ETFs and macro news?

Open thread • Replies: 77 • Updated: 2026-07-09 19:41 UTC

OP

Seems like every BTC pump or dump is blamed on ETF flows or macro headlines.

>Is that just the reality now, or are people overlooking other factors?

Early replies (first 3)

62450753
>>62450728
Its 100% cz dependent, just tradingview has to write something to the price action.

62450760
>>62450728
Btc does this thing where it lulls the newfag into a false sense of superiority. Both in bull and bear markets. In a bull market, it tricks newfags into thinking it will go up forever and traps them in overleveraged longs and in bear markets, it tricks them into it never going back up and they either sell low, sell covered calls or lever up short and get milked. Just hold spot and don't pay attention for a decade.

62450902
>>62450753
cz?

Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

62462709
>>62450728
>has the coin sabotaged by glowniggers into becoming a "store of value" instead of digital cash lost all popular support as well as reason to exist and is now entirely propped up by the very institutions that made sure it'll never be in competition with them become too reliant on said institutions?

What

62462717
>>62462593
I'm more interested in TBVs. That's where I think the bigger opportunity is.

62462723
>>62462709
Bitcoin has become more than just a payments network. That's not the same as losing its reason to exist.

62462739
>>62462709
If Bitcoin has "no reason to exist," why are people still building new infrastructure around it?

62462938
>>62462717
Because it's not just the tech anymore. Aegis and Utila getting involved tells me there's real interest in native BTC.

62462943
>>62462739
>building new infrastructure around it?
I think that's called Ethereum

62462946
>>62462943
not bitcoin?

62462953
>>62462943
ethereum is the king of it

62462965
>>62462946
Nothing is being built around or on top of Bitcoin. When people hear tokenized assets or stablecoins are being built on crypto they think crypto = Bitcoin, but everyone who understands even a bit about crypto knows this is not true. If someone disagrees, feel free to shill whatever Bitcoin has achieved in the past 1 year, or in the past 5 years for that matter. I can't think of anything besides inscriptions, which had some potential but ended up as nothing more than speculative meme-NFTs. Bitcoin isn't designed to keep up with fast moving crypto tech, it's designed to be solid and ossified. It's biggest achievement is having not changed at all, monetarily or technologically.

62463137
>>62462965
You're only looking at the base layer. A lot of the innovation is happening around Bitcoin, not by changing Bitcoin itself.

62463151
>>62463137
Such as? Besides Lightning, which is mostly just custodial wallets, there is not a single Bitcoin L2 that's used.
>but bro they are in development muh 2 weeks away
This has been the case for 5+ years now.

62463201
>>62463151
Stacks, Lombard and BoB

62463256
>>62463151
>>62463201
Babylon is worth looking into if you think nothing's being built around bitcoin.

62463258
What's the best thing to do? Buy or sell

62463262
>>62450728

62463263
>>62463201
WBTC TVL is 10x more than all of these combined. There's way more BTC lending happening on Ethereum than on BTC L2s.

62463265
>>62450728
ETF’s = whales

62463279
>>62459225
You are forgetting every nations currency have the strength of wet noodles now. They will print again soon.

62463285
bitcoin is a commodity backed ETF without the commodity

all it ever is and all it ever will be

62463301
There should be a 90s themed live action series called something cheesy like "Good morning Mr. Nakamoto" about the bitcoin creator. After making it big in his mid 20s he has become a multi billionaire, but is extremely paranoid so he decided to keep a low profile. Now he's in his late 30s, a family man with a couple kids, and the series follow an episodic format where he desperately tries to hide from his family his enormous wealth and just pretends to be an office worker. The thing is he has zero credentials so he can't actually get a job so it becomes incrementally difficult (due to plot inducement stupidity) to maintain the farce. Prying yet endearing secondary characters keep coming and leaving, and oftentimes misunderstandings make him almost blow his cover. During some weaker seasons he's stalked by some group of nerds that are almost sure he's the real deal and want to take revenge due to prices plunging, but eventually some shenanigan makes the nerds pin their suspicions on Nakamoto's rival (who was trying to cuck him with his wife, who by the way goes through a very annoying arc because she realizes his hubby is not being open with her or something). Nakamoto's personality is a bit like Hal from Malcolm in the middle but less lolrandom, he just plays as a fool to better hide his secret.

62463320
>>62463301
Some thematic episodes could include his wife hiring a financial advisor because he played the broke ass bit too much, and he tries to stop the advisor from peeking at his 100b net worth at any cost. He also gets in trouble because the advisor is not very bright and tells terrible advice very overconfidently, so Nakamoto counters with suspiciously good and sharp analysis (fortunately his wife just doesnt care because he has ingrained the dumb with money narrative and the advisor is very confident, and also a financial advisor duh). In another episode he gets pinned by the IRS after accidentally doing some billion level transaction and having to pay tenths of millions in taxes. Another one where he (again, Plot) is revealed to have all the money behind a 20 word seed, but he doesnt remember the words, so it's a musical where he finally manages to recall them through a catchy song. The episode is rated quite poorly though because it doesn't fit with the general tone. In the seventh and last season his younger kid finds out by dumb luck and the rest of that season is him preventing them from telling mom. The show gets cancelled afterwards due do decline in viewership and the plot never gets finished.

62463429
>>62463301
>>62463320
This has potential

62464033
>>62463263
but it doesn't mean it'll stay that way.

62464098
>>62463429
I could foresee an actual ending where he has to let all his earnings go because of some contrived dumb reason, but it's framed in a way that looks somewhat deep and poignant. His cover is blown in the process, but as he's revealed to be the creator of Bitcoin, he suddently gets a call from one of the companies he tried to apply the most though all the seasons (let's say it's a mid-sized company that offers an average salary, it has it's wacky employees and boss, they usually appear for like 10 minutes every few episodes just to reject Nakamoto). He's finally accepted and he merrily joins (it's a subtle joke because now that he's a public figure he could easily be gunning for 500k jobs, but he settles for this as a nudge to the audience), managing to achieve the life he pretended to have (and actually wanted) from the get go. The series end with him walking into the office's slightly rundown and shabby building with a bright smile. You even get to make that cheap overused allegory of ''choosing happiness and being true to your principles over riches''. I feel like there's an alternate universe where this would have killed it.

62464115
>>62463256
I'll keep testing TBVs on Aave V4. BTC has a lot more going on now than just being a store of value.

62464121
>>62463301
The best ending would be nobody ever finding out if it's really him. Keeping the mystery alive is probably the most Satoshi thing possible.

62464142
>>62464098
You're too invested in that drama. It's not happening in real life.

62464251
>>62464121
You know what? That's fair. I relied too much on 90s sitcom tropes. It should just end episodically, there's just no more seasons and that's it.
>>62464142
I don't have much going on in my life and my family and friends find my occurrences boring. And it's supposed to be a comedy. What drama? I want it to be goofy and for all publics!

62464760
>>62464033
Take stablecoins for another example. Zero. Zilch. Nada. $0.00 -- Remember when 100% of USDT used to be on Bitcoin? Now it's 0%, Omni is dead and Tether has moved to every other chain. There is nothing more to Bitcoin than BTC the asset.

62464820
I have a credit card that gives me cash back in the form of BTC. So my investment strategy is just skimming off my purchases anyway.

item.title: there is no meritocracy for financial freedom — 22 replies — 88.0/hr
item.content:encoded:
there is no meritocracy for financial freedom

Open thread • Replies: 22 • Updated: 2026-07-09 20:02 UTC

OP

>the intelligent elite few that figured it out at 14 get into ivy leagues are guaranteed high-paying jobs straight out of graduation (HFE, high-finance, big tech)
>permanent job security, ivy league education holds permanent prestige
>the best college experience possible
>all paid for too
and
>they're all cheaters now
wow.

Early replies (first 3)

62464304
>>62464271
>guaranteed high-paying jobs straight out of graduation
I went to Brown for undergrad and this was very much false. Even with a chemistry degree, which isn’t the best choice but better than a lot of shit. Had to get a PhD before I could start making ok money. The only way to make money straight out of an Ivy League bachelors in chemistry was to go into consulting, which to be fair is not an option for most people with bachelors degrees in chemistry from less “prestigious” schools. Unfortunately I was not charismatic enough to land a consulting job, so grad school hell was my next best option.

62464305
>>62464304
>start making ok money
ahh, "ok money". humble, I respect that.

62464330
>>62464271
You can call it cheating or whatever but legitimately there is no reason to think for yourself anymore in a large variety of capacities. I don’t think this is necessarily a good thing, but thinking for yourself as your first step vs approaching AI as your first step will slow you down dramatically and if anything this image is proof positive. These kids probably did literally zero self thinking and got answers likely near instantaneously and they were by and large the RIGHT answers vs literally spending hundreds of hours learning and studying and then another 2 hours trying to interpret the questions and use whatever knowledge you might have retained to answer them, largely erroneously.

I’m 41 years old, have a high paying job, mostly from being someone who can think critically and problem solve very well. Every time I even start to think for myself at this point I realize how retarded that is. I get probably 50x more done now going straight to AI off the bat, no exaggeration, and I’m doing jobs in a day that would have taken me YEARS just to learn to do previously. It’s fucking insane. I still do have to think for myself when the AI is clearly doing something dumb, but only long enough to get it back on track. At this point I barely even check what it’s doing, just review the output because 9/10 times it has a solid plan and reviewing every plan in detail was slowing me down.

Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

62464333
>>62464305
What would you define as “ok money?” My first job out of my PhD I was making $90k. But that was in a high COL area, so I was making about 30% less than the median household income for the area I was living in. I guess that’s decent for someone in their late 20s but it’s not like I was rolling in money.

62464346
>>62464333

62464394
>>62464346

62464404

62464424
>>62464330

62464460
If this is the first time you've realized that maybe meritocracy as sold by capitalism is a lie then your pretty fucking dumb

62464470
>>62464394
ayo you sound cool man :), I appreciate it.

off the top of my head:

1. why don't phds have their own online forum to congregate? with their accredited credentials + identity being the ticket, would this serve any purpose beyond an email chain? (wouldn't replace it, but would it be bloat?) this wouldn't aim to replace conferences of course either
2. in research, is there a dilemma of any sort for competition? like perhaps there could be cooperation, but given the natural order of things there is an incentive to be the ones to get it "first" or "on your own" (again im not a research guy, this is coming from ignorance); not exactly a prisoner's dilemma but something more akin to winner-takes-all maybe
3. favorite ice cream flavor?

62464471
>>62464460
>your pretty fucking dumb

62464480
>>62464471
>SAAAR YOU MUST USE THE PROPER CONTRACTIONS, APOLIGIZE TO THE ROBOT NOW
No. Kys

62464486
>>62464480
i'm being saar'd? does that mean you just realized for the first time that meritocracy was sold by capitalism as a lie?

62464492
>>62464486
That is a nonsensical statement. I'm saar'ing you because complaining about AI and then acting as a human spell check is a silly trait expected out of a silly person.

62464497
>>62464492
what else do you take so seriously in life?

62464524
>>62464480
>no um you’re actually the jeet

Yeah okay Patel, we believe you

62464541
>>62464470
Kek these are some big questions but I’ll give me two cents.

1) There are alumni networks at these schools that serve this sort of purpose but afaik not many people use them except for trying to get jobs. It seems to me like most people do just fine without them, so it ends up only being people who are struggling and not enough people trying to help. Most of the extent to which your connections at these schools helps is only through people you know personally through your time there. Otherwise I think people are hesitant to vouch for someone just because they went to the same school, although I do think that can give you an upper edge. One of my rec letters for grad school came from a professor of mine who did her PhD with the department head of the grad school I was applying to, and one of the interviewers for my first job did his PhD in the same lab as me, albeit about 25 years earlier. As to why there’s no one online forum to congregate, I think it’s probably the same answer as to why people shy away from the alumni portals. Most people are doing well enough, so they’re not going to go there unless they need help.

2) For sure at the grad school / post doc level there is. Depending on how the PI runs the lab, it can be very cutthroat which often hurts the output of the lab more than it motivates people. At the end of the day, even the best-and-brightest grad students are still very early in their scientific careers and usually don’t have good ideas of their own (myself included) so if the lab is struggling to produce compelling results, people get very territorial about their projects. Lab to lab and university to university there’s also a ton of competition for funding, and you have to be “the first” to do something big to secure a big grant. In the current environment, grant money is tighter than ever since it mostly comes from the government, so I can only imagine things have gotten even worse since I was in school

62464556
>>62464470
Hit the word limit on my last reply lol. I was going to add that now I work in private industry (pharma) which is also very competitive since everyone wants to develop the next big product, and there is still competition between individuals for promotions and whatnot but it’s a very different ball game since the goal isn’t publishing papers and securing grants like it is in academia. I work at a large company which is nice because we have a cushion of cash and existing products to keep the lights on when things maybe aren’t progressing so well in research, but currently they are increasing pressure on us to get something to market. Otherwise there may be cuts. But generally the atmosphere between colleagues is more chill and collaborative than academia. I think if money was tighter or if my department had cuts in the future, it will be a different story. Moderna is a good example of a company that grew too fast and shrunk just as quickly, and I have heard the work culture there is atrocious.

3) maybe the toughest question of all, but I love me a pistachio

62464559
>>62464460
>your pretty fucking dumb
vgm sire

62464600
>>62464424

62464905
>>62464600
Fair enough
I was maybe a bit harsh

62464920
>>62464271

The cure to the affordability crisis is mass production of physical goods and services. I've already gone over this, but I have zero power and nobody gives a shit anyways. You can't tax your way out of this. You can't loan/print your way out of this. You can't "muh labor unions & eat the rich" your way out of this. And you can't "Luigi" your way out of this.

You also can't regulate your way out of this. Regulations are a major cause for all of this in the first place. They make it nearly impossible to build power plants or utility stations, they make it nearly impossible to build housing anywhere, they make it nearly impossible to experiment with new drugs, they make it nearly impossible to build new cars and they give "free" money to 18 year old tards that don't understand budgeting, while letting university administrators (95% Democrat) hike tuition fees to infinity. This is why everyone is either creating software apps on phones, crypto, or other tech related shit, because it's one of the least regulated industries.

You need mass production, which means:
- Eliminate 90% of current regulations in every industry.
- Eliminate all required hiring practices and have complete freedom of association.
- Eliminate 90% of taxes/tariffs and allow both corporations and consumers to decide where their money flows. The only things we should be funding are military, courts and direct consumer subsidies to those with actually physical and mental health handicaps, not the scams we have today.
- Serious crackdown on activist judges and radically harsher penalties for criminals.
- Privatize Social Security and turn it into a forced private savings program, like a 401k, that's only accessible at age 65+. People should control their retirement, not the government.

Anybody that is truly impoverished and cannot fund an HSA should get a direct subsidy from the government into their account. This way they still get to choose their insurance plan and the insurance providers still need to compete for their business.
- Deport all illegal aliens.

kind of fucked
your best bet if you have a car is to get a job working overnight at McDonalds or another 24 hour fast food place
if you aren't obviously a druggy or look like a psycho they will hire you on the spot, seriously
or just lie on your resume and get any menial wageslave job
just research some businesses that used to exist in your area, for example find restaurants that went out of business and say you worked as a dishwasher
if they ask about gaps in your resume you can say you were looking after a sick family member
make everything before the last 5 years or so a small business that you owned and operated yourself, for example lawnmowing

so your resume should look like this
graduated high school
worked for yourself as a believable small business like a lawnmower
"my business was destroyed by covid"
began looking after a sick relative for 2-3 years
worked as a dishwasher, or cleaning toilets, or some other thing for a business like a hotel that went out of business recently in your town or a nearby town. for 2 or so years until 6-12 months ago when that business closed
been searching for a job since then

problems with this method:
- if they check references you're fucked but no one does for these dead end jobs like McDonalds maintainence or dishwasher. there are services you can hire which pretend to be references for you but it shouldn't be necessary
- if you don't have a car you're just fucked, you need it to have a leg up on other applicants for these jobs. I'm pretty sure I was hired by McDonalds the moment I said I had my own vehicle and could be available to start at 2am
- if you get the job you're trapped in a hell dead end wage slave job

source: same situation as you OP but I was 29 rather than 39
